speaker: interviewer
text: October 11, 1995. Survivor Israel Berkenwald. This is tape two.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well when hell started, when the Germans came, grabbing people in the middle of the street, taking them to all kind of jobs that was terribly humiliating, my father decided-- he called my brother and me into the house, and he says, look, you're big enough to realize that there's danger here. Now, a lot of people are going to go over to the Russian zone. Because, as you know, Ribbentrop and Stalin wrote a pact. They became friends all of a sudden. So they divided Poland in half, and the border was a river called Bug, B-U-G. And since a lot of youngsters and older people started running away from the Germans into the Russian zone, my father decided that the crown of his life, his two sons, since my three sisters were married already, just let them save their lives and run into the Russian zone. He gave us-- each one of us had five gold coins, Russian gold coins that somehow he had it. I never knew he had them. But they were sewn in by my mother into the belts of our pants. We had, each one of us, my brother and I, had five gold Russian coins, so that when we get into the Russian zone, we should be able to buy food, or even buy our lives with it, if need be. Well, we took the train. We had to rig a wagon. Finally at night, we came in front of the river Bug, which was the borderline between German-- the German army and the Russian army. And you had to buy somebody. In other words, you had a lot of Poles used to have handmade boats. What do you call wooden things that you put together trees-- cut of trees-- and tied together--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a barge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a barge, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ferry, like a ferry.

speaker: interviewee
text: A ferry is a boat. It skips my mind right now, but it was a handmade barge made out of trees, and that is the way he crossed people from the German border into the Russian border. Unfortunately, that was a night-- by the way, where I live here, I have two of my friends that were with me on the same trip. One of them, two brothers also. One of them went right back. One of them crossed with me. What happened, the Germans and the Russians were playing patsy at that time. That's the only thing I could call it. It's either patsy or catch the wolf. They were shooting at the barges of whoever tried to cross the Bug. And it was a very severe winter, and there was already chunks of ice floating in the river. And I had a brother that was 6' 1'. He was a very tall boy. And while he was standing, whether it was a few bullets from a machine gun or one bullet, he got shot in the head. And the top of his head came off completely, and he just buckled over and went under water. And that friend of ours, a neighbor that lives here too-- we are still friends-- didn't let me. I went to jump in and save him. I happened to be a good swimmer. He didn't let me go. He says, forget it, he's dead. And I was really in a panic, and I was-- I must have been in very bad shape, because I just listened to him like I was either mesmerized or totally stoned.

speaker: interviewer
text: But don't you think he was right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was right. I realized later, once his head was off, he was dead. And I lost my brother, and we crossed the border. And from there, we went to Bialystok. That friend of mine that lives here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bialystok, which at the time that was occupied by the Russians. We were already in the Soviet Union, although it was Poland. But it was Soviet Union as far as Stalin was concerned, because Poland was cut in half and divided between Germany and Poland. I came to Bialystok, and I didn't have it too good. However, I was able to cash a gold coin, buy some food. I slept in a synagogue on the floor. And next day, I decided to leave Bialystok to go further. My father had told us eventually, he may follow us. And it took me about two days to reach a town. Also I didn't walk too much. I mostly went by horse and wagon. I gave the farmer some money, and he took me until he went to his village, and then I'd jump off and go to another one. In some cases, I just hitched a ride in the wagon that the farmer shouldn't see me. And I came into a town, Novogorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: Novogorod?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novogorod. It's called really, in Polish, it's called [POLISH]. I was there, and somehow I was able to get some food over there-- not legally, illegally-- and I was there for a couple of months. And that was also a town that was a passing stage for a lot of people. We were called [RUSSIAN]. [RUSSIAN] in Russian means that you left one part of the country for another. So I was [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: But I met some people from Lodz and I kept on inquiring, what's happening in Lodz, what's happening in Lodz? And I met some people that knew my father. I was sure that they knew my father, although they didn't tell me. Because if I asked them about it, they sort of didn't give me a right answer, which gave me a kind of chill in my chest. I felt very unsure. What are they not telling me? It's not what they're telling me. What are they not telling me? And right then I decided-- that was at the beginning of March-- 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided-- 1940-- I decided I must go back to Lodz and see what's happening to my family. I must tell them about my brother. I can't be here alone. And then the Russians were giving out constantly orders for all the [RUSSIAN] that came from the German side to register and go into Siberia, where we'll be safe. And a lot of people did, and it saved their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these [RUSSIAN] were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN]. Yeah, they were from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jews? They were all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Jews, and they were from Poland, from different towns. And some of them were from my town. But from my town, they people that I asked didn't give me a clear answer, and what they didn't tell me made me worry. And I figured I better go back and see what's going on. Maybe they need me. Now I'm the oldest man in the family. And I smuggled myself into Bialystok, and from Bialystok, I smuggled myself in across the Bug into the German lines. I'm saying this in two, three minutes, but believe me, it was a lifetime for me. Just to go back and cross a river and get in from the Russian zone into the German zone was a hell of a time and a hell of a risk, and I made it. And from there, I went into Warsaw. In Warsaw, I saw terrible things that happened. The ghetto wasn't on yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What terrible things did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw Jews were walking around like-- like they were zombies. I saw already hunger. I saw lines for bread. I saw people taken for all kind of work. The fear of Germans was terrible. On top of it, they were wearing white bands with the blue-- white armbands with blue star of Davids on it. Oh my god. Now you can run away, now you haven't got anything. I haven't got a white band. Anyway, I left Warsaw, and from Warsaw to Lodz, I knew already how to get back, because there was only one highway. The highway, I couldn't travel, but on the side of the highway, I could travel. And from Warsaw to Lodz was a town called Glowno, which I had a relative living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there to Glowno, and I met the man, and he fed me and he clothed me and he said, go back to Russia, because here, you're not going to last long. I said, nope, I have to go back and see what's happening to my parents. Whether they knew or didn't, I don't know, what happened to my parents. But they told me and gave me good advice, go back to Soviet Union and you'll save your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm going back to Glowno. From Glowno, about 27 or 28 kilometers was another town called Strykow, which I had another relative living, and that one was on my mother's side. I knew where they were living. I went in there, I inquired about my parents. They couldn't tell me anything. But they also advised me, go back to the Soviet Union if you can. If you know the road, go back. And I said, no, I'm not going back, I'm going to find out what's happening to my parents. I left Stryhow. From Stryhow to Lodz was not-- about 9, 10 miles by foot. And I came to Lodz. I see barbed wires is all around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Lodz get a--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was April-- April 2 or April 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the Warsaw ghetto hadn't started yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Warsaw ghetto was being built.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Lodz ghetto was made first?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Lodz ghetto was already there because November 1, Germany declared Lodz, Poland as part of the Third Reich. It became the Third Reich November 1. So therefore-- therefore, it was German territory. And they already built a ghetto and had wire fences all around the ghetto. For me to get into the ghetto would be risky, because every 100 yards was a German soldier-- actually, not a German soldier. It was a German soldier, but they were from the Ukraine. And I couldn't possibly get into the ghetto without risking my life. So I decided I could do it since I knew the cemetery, head of the cemetery, [? Chirminsky ?] before the war. He was Jewish. A Jewish cemetery which was also the border of the ghetto. And I used to go there quite often to play with my friend. I figured if I get to the cemetery, I'll be able to get in to the ghetto. And that's exactly what I did. I wasn't easy. It was very hard, believe me. A whole night it took me to get over that fence, and I did. I made it. And since it was still dark, I was afraid to go into town. So I spent the rest of the night in the cemetery, the Jewish cemetery. And then I heard already voices, so I figured it was getting light. So I started walking towards the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went into the house where my friend lived, and his father took care of the Jewish cemetery. They weren't there. There were different people. So they asked me, where are you from? I said, I'm from Lodz, I live in a certain town. They said, you can't live over there. Jews don't live there no more. Jews live here. And if they find you here without a yellow star, they'll shoot you, or the Jewish police might take you into jail. I said, well, have you got a yellow star that I could use? He says no, but they gave me some yellow paper which they pinned on front and back of my jacket. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm in the ghetto. I can't go to the house where I lived. So I had an aunt living in a street which was called [NON-ENGLISH] 8, my father's youngest sister. And I figured I'll go in there, because that was part of the ghetto. The bridges were not built yet in the ghetto. What you had to do is wait for the gate to open, and the gate opened like every 15 minutes. When there were enough people to go from one part of the ghetto to the other, the gate opened. But they were building already a bridge to cross over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to my aunt. That's the only place I knew in this part of Lodz that I could find someone I know. I come into my aunt's house, my aunt isn't there, my uncle isn't there. My oldest sister and her husband is there. I walk in and I say, what's happening? They told me the whole story, that now they live in the ghetto. I said where is mom and dad? And they started crying and telling me that the part of town where we lived was relocated in the middle of the night and my niece, nine-year-old niece, was with my parents that night, because they were afraid that in their part where they lived, only about five minutes away from us, but it was next to the German police station. So in order to keep my niece safe, she slept in my house with my father and mother. Well, we all know what happened. That was the night that they relocated the building where my parents lived. They took my father and mother and my niece away. What do you mean by relocated?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they called it, relocated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it really mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not let them stay in this house, though, because this house was assigned for Germans. It was a beautiful building in the heart of town. So what they did, about 10,000 people, I think, that they caught that night and they took them out and they send them to a different town. Some of them run away, some of them got shot. My parents wound up in a town near [INAUDIBLE] called [NON-ENGLISH]. But we didn't know that until about a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I says, where is Doris and the baby? She lived someplace else. And where is my youngest sister, Rose? Rose is living here, and they'll be here pretty soon, but they went to work. They got a job in a factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, let me go and see the baby, my sister's baby, the young one, the boy. So I-- again, I had to go through, and now my sister and brother gave me yellow star I should wear. And I went to my sister whose husband was a doctor. And they had a better apartment, because of him being a doctor. He was sort sought after by the men that led the Jewish ghetto. His name was Chaim Rumkowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the doctors were sort of a privileged crowd. There weren't too many, but as many as there were, they were privileged. So she had a two-room apartment and a kitchen, and also a toilet in the house. Unfortunately a few months later, they closed it, because a Jew wasn't allowed this luxury, so she had to use an outhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: But since she had more room and she loved it if I should be with the baby, and I slept in the same room where my-- where my little nephew was. So I lived with my sister and with my brother-in-law for two years in that apartment. [SIGHS] Unfortunately, I'm jumping ahead of myself, but since I have to finish with my sister, what happened was that there was a very big factory in a town near Lodz called Zelow. There were about 8,000 or 9,000 people working in the factory, and they had no medical care. So the-- the head of the Jewish ghetto asked some doctors if they would volunteer to go to a different town where they get good lodging and good care, and we were all taken care of because he'll be the doctor for that factory. My brother-in-law jumped at this occasion, because the ghetto was really no place to bring up a child. How little did they know. So they went. However, they couldn't take anybody else. I couldn't go with them. So back I went to my oldest sister, and I lived with my oldest sister and brother-in-law. And they left for Zelow, where they had it pretty good for like three or four months. That was 1942-- the end of 1942. But what happened after a couple of months, the factory in Zelow got an order to close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they make there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were making clothes, winter clothes for the German soldiers that fought in Russia. White clothes. White jackets and hoods, white shoes, and the pants were going over the shoes. So they sort of blended in into the snow while fighting in the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they closed the factory, they took every able man from Zelow, they took to send them to Lodz, where I was. My brother-in-law and my sister and the baby, they didn't let them go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They demanded my sister and my brother-in-law could go, but the baby they must give up. So they wouldn't want to, and my brother-in-law was a little bit hard under the head at that time. He said, I'm not giving up my wife or my kid. You brought me here, you send me back. Well, you play with that, you play with Germans. And he started fighting with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened their machine gun and shot my sister, my brother-in-law and the baby. Now, when the people that came from Zelow into the Lodz ghetto next day, the ones that were able to work, told us what had happened to the doctor. That was my sister and my brother-in-law and the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister, Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora, yeah. So naturally, we didn't take the news too kindly. We were heartbroken, but we had to live. Now we had to worry about my parents and the niece. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't hear anything from them. We didn't know whether they were living or not. We knew that they are in Zawichost. However, Zawichost was being made judenrein, and everyone in Zawichost was sent to Chelmno. Chelmno was also a little town between Zawichose and Lodz where the Germans started to kill Jews in trucks by gas. They used to asphyxiate them. What they did is they put in 50, 60 people into a truck, lock the truck tight. And then the driver used to press on the pedal very hard for about 20 or 30 minutes, and whoever was in the truck was dead within a half an hour. And then they were all buried either in a ravine or the workers dug a common grave, and that's where they were buried. And this is where my parents died, in Chelmno, and my niece. Now this, we didn't know until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: However, we knew that in Chelmno bad things are happening because a friend of mine who is living now in Israel. His name is Chaim Noymillier. In fact, he's a policeman in Israel. He came into the ghetto. He smuggled himself into the ghetto in 1942 and told us crazy stories about these things. He told us crazy stories. And we all thought that he's out of his mind. Something is absolutely wrong with that man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean he told you about the gas trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us that they gassing people, and they're burying thousands of people in common graves. And he says in some instances they put bodies between wood and they burn them. And we actually felt sorry for him. He worked in our kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we felt sorry for him, my god, he must have been through something very bad to come tell stories like this. It's impossible. It's impossible. Unfortunately after the war, we found out what-- I was in Zawichost after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what happened to my parents. You mentioned he was your kommando. That was the work kommando you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the work kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job at a warehouse that were receiving vegetables for the ghetto, and we were unloading either trucks or wagons with a trolley car. Came wagons with food, and we were-- they were dragging the wagons. And we were unloading them and then cleaned them up and send them back-- or trucks. And this was the kommando that I worked, and I got a job like this in the ghetto. But every time there was a lot of death in the ghetto, they used to call me out for two or three days, sometimes a week. I worked in the cemetery digging graves. Now, digging graves in the summertime in Lodz was not too bad. It wasn't a pleasant job, but it wasn't bad. But if I was taken out from my job to go and dig graves wintertime, with the earth up to about a foot was like steel. I used to dig a grave with a pick, and you had to make two graves a day. It wasn't easy. It wasn't easy. So many a times I worked in a cemetery digging graves, and then I went back to my regular job.

speaker: interviewer
text: That job of unloading the vegetables sounds like it was a pretty good job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, it was a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay to get that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took people that could do the work. It was very hard work. Very hard work. I mean, you took two guys that had to unload a wagon full of vegetables, but there were five, 10 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people get these vegetables, or did they end up with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The Germans send this in for the people that worked in the ghetto. You must realize the Lodz ghetto had about 200, 300-- oh, 200, maybe, I don't know, about 200 factories that helped the war efforts for the Germans. We made uniforms, we made shoes, we made jackets, we made hats. All kind of factories. Then we made leather stuff. We made an awful lot of things in the Lodz ghetto. There was an awful lot of factories. Mostly they were garment factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that why they kept it going until 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why they kept that going, yes. As a matter of fact, I'll just jump out into 1944 for one second. When they closed the first factory called the Gonick factory that made ladies uniforms, we knew we were in trouble. The factory's name was Gonick, G-O-N-I-C-K. The Gonick factory. When they closed that factory, we realized that something bad is on the way. Now, let me go back now to when I came back from Russia. I came back from Russia, it was in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1940. And in June, we had a very important visit in the Lodz ghetto, Mr. Heinrich Himmler. I think, if I'm not mistaken, it was June 7. He came to visit the Lodz ghetto and also inspect two factories, two tailor factories. One he inspected one day, the other one he went back and came back the next day and he inspected. And then unfortunately or fortunately, I got a-- a glimpse of that devil. He came in an open car. In an open car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that he was coming, because the announcement was there that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that he was part of Hitler's circle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But at the same time, if he came to visit the Lodz ghetto and-- and look at factories, I knew that we got it made. Maybe we'll be able to live through it, since such a high-ranking Nazi comes to visit the ghetto in Lodz.
